name: Lebanese Arabic (Beiruti)

ancestors.notes: French adstrate (Mandate-era and ongoing): heavy loanword stratum, /ʒ/ reinforced, code-switching.

notes: Lebanese Arabic — Beiruti urban koine, a South-Central Levantine variety. INPUT CONTRACT: fully-diacritized Arabic. ق qaf → /ʔ/ (urban; rural/Bedouin /ɡ/ via the Levantine parent). ج jim → /ʒ/. Interdentals merged in urban speech (θ→/t/, ð→/d/, ðˤ→/dˤ/). DISTINGUISHING FEATURE: STRONG imāla — Classical /aː/ raised to [eː] except adjacent to emphatic/guttural consonants, and final /-a/ (ة) raised to [e]; modelled here as allophone_rules (LB_IMALA_*) that compose after the inherited emphasis-backing rules. This pervasive vowel raising is the most salient Lebanese marker. The five-long / three-short vowel system and /aj aw/→[eː oː] monophthongization are inherited from the Levantine parent. French is a heavy adstrate.

sources.author: Almbark, R. & Hellmuth, S.
sources.title: Acoustic analysis of the Syrian Arabic vowel system
sources.notes: READ in full. Damascus/Syrian vowel system: five long /iː eː aː oː uː/, three short contrastive /i a u/; mid short [e o] and schwa are allophones of /i u/ (§3-4). Mid-long /eː oː/ from /aj aw/ coalescence (§1.1). /aː/ higher/fronter in Coastal Syria than Damascus (p.2).

sources.author: Fadda, H.
sources.title: Language Variation in Western Amman
sources.publisher: MA thesis, Canada Institute of Linguistics (CanIL)
sources.notes: READ (phonology chapter). Ammani koine consonants = urban Palestinian [ʔ t d dˤ ʒ], 'identical to other Levantine cities' (pp.27-28). Qaf labels (Al-Wer & Herin 2011:60): [g] gilit, [q] qeltu, [ʔ] Jerusalem, [k] rural West Bank. Vowels: short /i u a/, long /iː uː eː oː aː/ (Al-Wer 2011:4). Diphthongs /aj aw/→[eː oː] except before glide: bajt→beːt, sˤawt→sˤoːtˤ (p.30).

sources.author: Cowell, M.W.
sources.title: A Reference Grammar of Syrian Arabic (Based on the Dialect of Damascus)
sources.notes: Standard Damascus grammar. Primary text NOT obtained (Internet Archive borrow-only, no full text); its Damascene vowel classification (5 long / 5 short + schwa) is cited here only as reported and tested by Almbark & Hellmuth 2015 §1.3.

sources.author: Watson, J.C.E.
sources.title: The Phonology and Morphology of Arabic
sources.notes: Emphasis (pharyngealization) spreading onto neighbouring low vowels. Not obtained in full; cited for the widely-described emphasis-spreading process.

allophone_rules.notes: Imāla is blocked adjacent to a guttural (pharyngeal/uvular/glottal) consonant: /aː/ stays [aː] after /ħ ʕ h ʔ x ɣ q/. Identity rule ordered before LB_IMALA_RAISE_AA so those environments do not raise (Lebanese imāla applies 'unless adjacent to emphatic or guttural consonants').

Imāla blocked before a guttural consonant: /aː/ stays [aː]. Companion of LB_IMALA_BLOCK_GUTT_AFTER. Emphatic environments are already handled by the inherited AR_LEV_EMPH_BACK_AA_* rules (ordered earlier), which back /aː/→[ɑː].

Strong Lebanese imāla: Classical /aː/ is fronted and raised to [eː] outside emphatic/guttural contexts (e.g. باب bāb → [beːb]). Grounded in the Coastal-vs-Damascus /aː/ fronting Almbark & Hellmuth 2015 (p.2) report, and the [æ]→[eː] raising Fadda 2016:52 documents for the region. The specifically Beiruti magnitude of the raise rests additionally on secondary description (see notes/sources) — Naïm's Beirut grammar was not obtained.

Word-final /-a/ (incl. tāʔ marbūṭa ة) raises to [e], the most consistent Lebanese imāla context. Fires on the final segment only. (The base ar spec surfaces both the preceding fatḥa and the tāʔ marbūṭa vowel, a pre-existing orthographic artifact; this rule raises the final segment only.)
